sections.title: Use
items: Situations or states that began in the past and continue now
Actions that happened at some point in the past when the exact time isn't given
Past actions whose results affect the present moment

sections.title: Examples
items: Mr Torres **has taught** at this school for over ten years.
I **have never tried** sushi — this will be the first time!
We can't go out — Mum **has lost** her keys.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: for
since
just
already
yet
ever
never
It's the first time

sections.title: Be careful
items: We don't use the present perfect simple when we want to say **when** something happened in the past. We use the past simple: ✓ I **did** my homework **last night**.
We don't use the past simple when we want to show that something happened before now or is still important now. We use the present perfect simple: ✓ I've **finished**! Can I go home now?
Some verbs have irregular past participle forms.

sections.title: Use
items: Activities that have been going on up to the present or have just stopped

sections.title: Examples
items: I **have been waiting** here for half an hour — where have you been?
She looks exhausted because she **has been running**.

sections.title: Useful notes
rows: I've been learning English **for** over three years.
I've **just** been reading the school newspaper.

sections.title: Be careful
items: The present perfect simple often emphasises the result of an action: ✓ She's **written** an article for the school newspaper. (= She's finished it.)

The present perfect continuous often emphasises the action, and the time spent on the action, rather than the result: ✓ She's **been writing** an article for the school newspaper. (= She's started, but she hasn't finished it yet.)

exercises.title: A: Present perfect simple
exercises.instructions: Complete using the correct present perfect simple form of the verbs in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete using the correct present perfect simple form of the verbs in brackets.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Already shows the project is complete with a result that matters now — use have finished.

wrongOptions.finished: Missing auxiliary have — present perfect needs have + past participle.
wrongOptions.finish: The bare infinitive (finish) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Emily ...... (live) in this town all her life.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: All her life links past to present, so Emily still lives there: has lived.

wrongOptions.lived: Missing auxiliary has — third person singular present perfect needs has + past participle.
wrongOptions.live: The bare infinitive (live) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Today is unfinished time, so use present perfect negative haven't seen.

wrongOptions.not / see: The bare infinitive (not / see) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

wrongOptions.had: Missing auxiliary have — present perfect needs have + past participle.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

wrongOptions.you ever eaten: Missing auxiliary have — present perfect needs have + past participle.
wrongOptions.you / ever / eat: The bare infinitive (you / ever / eat) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: They ...... (not / choose) a name for the puppy yet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is yet in negatives, so the correct answer is "haven't chosen".

wrongOptions.not / choose: The bare infinitive (not / choose) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

wrongOptions.sophie just arrived: Missing auxiliary has — third person singular present perfect needs has + past participle.
wrongOptions.sophie / just / arrive: The bare infinitive (sophie / just / arrive) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: I've never visited — experience up to now; before fits present perfect, not past simple.

wrongOptions.B: "I" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.I: "I" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

optionContrast.I've: "I've" doesn't fit here → "I've" does, because I've never visited — experience up to now; before fits present perfect, not past simple.

optionContrast.I: "I" doesn't fit here → "I've" does, because it is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

exercises.title: C: Write sentences
exercises.instructions: Look at the picture and use the prompts to write sentences. Use the correct form of the present perfect simple.
exercises.studentInstruction: Look at the picture and use the prompts to write sentences. Use the correct form of the present perfect simple.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Yet goes at the end of a negative present perfect sentence: hasn't started yet.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: film / not / start / yet → The film hasn't started yet.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — has/have + past participle; yet goes at the end of negatives.

wrongOptions.not: "not" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.start: Conjugate "start" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "start" in the answer.
wrongOptions.yet: "yet" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "start" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

items.answer: Dad has already bought the popcorn.
acceptedAnswers: Dad has already bought the popcorn.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Already shows the popcorn is bought before now, so the sentence is "Dad has already bought the popcorn.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: Dad / already / buy / the popcorn → Dad has already bought the popcorn.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — has/have + past participle; yet goes at the end of negatives.

wrongOptions.already: "already" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.buy: Conjugate "buy" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "buy" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "buy" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

items.answer: The children have just sat down.
acceptedAnswers: The children have just sat down.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Just + present perfect, so the sentence is "The children have just sat down.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: the children / just / sit down → The children have just sat down.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — has/have + past participle; yet goes at the end of negatives.

wrongOptions.just: "just" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.sit down: Conjugate "sit down" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "sit down" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "sit down" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Negative present perfect + yet, so the sentence is "Mia hasn't finished her drink.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: Mia / not / finish / her drink → Mia hasn't finished her drink.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — has/have + past participle; yet goes at the end of negatives.

wrongOptions.not: "not" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.finish: Conjugate "finish" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "finish" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "finish" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

items.answer: Tom has already turned off his phone.
acceptedAnswers: Tom has already turned off his phone.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Already before the past participle, so the sentence is "Tom has already turned off his phone.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: Tom / already / turn off / his phone → Tom has already turned off his phone.

wrongOptions.already: "already" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.turn off: Conjugate "turn off" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "turn off" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "turn off" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.

items.displayPrompt: someone / drop / a ticket / on the floor
items.answer: Someone has dropped a ticket on the floor.
acceptedAnswers: Someone has dropped a ticket on the floor.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Unspecified past time with present result, so the sentence is "Someone has dropped a ticket on the floor.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: someone / drop / a ticket / on the floor → Someone has dropped a ticket on the floor.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Combine the cue words in standard English word order and conjugate verbs to match the time clues.

wrongOptions.drop: Conjugate "drop" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "drop" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "drop" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

items.displayPrompt: they / not / pick it up / yet
items.answer: They haven't picked it up yet.
acceptedAnswers: They haven't picked it up yet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Negative + yet, so the sentence is "They haven't picked it up yet.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: they / not / pick it up / yet → They haven't picked it up yet.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — has/have + past participle; yet goes at the end of negatives.

wrongOptions.not: "not" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.pick it up: Conjugate "pick it up" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "pick it up" in the answer.
wrongOptions.yet: "yet" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "pick it up" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

exercises.title: D: Present perfect continuous
exercises.instructions: Complete using the correct present perfect continuous form of the verbs in brackets. Use short forms where possible.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete using the correct present perfect continuous form of the verbs in brackets. Use short forms where possible. Confirm each gap one by one.
exercises.passage:
Lucy: Hi Ben! You look tired. What (1) ...... (you / do)?
Ben: Hey Lucy. Well, (2) ...... (I / revise) for my maths test.
Lucy: Really? (3) ...... (you / study) all day?
Ben: Pretty much. (4) ...... (I / sit) in the library since this morning and (5) ...... (I / not / take) any breaks.
Lucy: That sounds awful!
Ben: Yeah, but (6) ...... (I / work) with Alex, so it's been a bit easier.
Lucy: Well, my sister and (7) ...... (I / clean) the house all afternoon.
Ben: Fun!
Lucy: Actually, yes! And (8) ...... (we / also / prepare) dinner for tonight.
Ben: Nice. What are you making?
Lucy: Pizza! We can't decide on the toppings, so (9) ...... (we / look) at recipes online.
Ben: Sounds great. By the way, (10) ...... (I / think) about joining the school basketball team.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is lucy asks what Ben has been doing recently, so "have you been doing" fits this gap.

wrongOptions.you been doing: Missing the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle or been + -ing.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple or present simple misses the ongoing link — use have/has been + -ing for continuous duration.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: The verb in brackets shows the base form — conjugate it to match the subject and tense clues in the line.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect continuous describes an action that started in the past and is still ongoing or recently finished.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ben is still revising, so "I've been revising" fits this gap.

wrongOptions.been revising: Missing the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle or been + -ing.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple or present simple misses the ongoing link — use have/has been + -ing for continuous duration.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Duration phrases (all day, since, for) often signal present perfect or continuous forms.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect continuous describes an action that started in the past and is still ongoing or recently finished.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is all-day study, so "Have you been studying" fits this gap.

wrongOptions.you been studying: Missing the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle or been + -ing.

gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Duration phrases (all day, since, for) often signal present perfect or continuous forms.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect continuous describes an action that started in the past and is still ongoing or recently finished.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: E: Circle the correct word or phrase
exercises.title: E: Circle the correct word or phrase
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

items.originalSentence: I think I've **seen / been seeing** that actor before.
items.completedSentence: I think I've seen that actor before.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "seen" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice uses a form of be + past participle. Match the tense of be to the time clue (each year → present, yesterday → past, etc.).

wrongOptions.been seeing: "been seeing" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("that actor before.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for by + agent and time clues — they tell you which passive tense fits.

optionContrast.been seeing: "been seeing" doesn't fit here → "seen" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("that actor before.").

items.originalSentence: She hasn't **finished / been finishing** her project yet.
items.completedSentence: She hasn't finished her project yet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "finished" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.been finishing: "been finishing" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("her project yet.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Yet goes at the end of negative present perfect sentences.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: She's been learning Spanish ...... three years.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
